Key Findings

What the data shows

Five movements define the story: a real but slowing decline, a rising care burden, working treatment, an uneven map, and clear priorities.

New infections vs. deaths, 2000–2024

New infections fell 59% (2.40M→0.98M) and deaths ~70% from the 2004 peak.

But the curve is flattening — 2023→2024 was almost flat (+0.25%). Prevention gains are plateauing.

People living with HIV rose from 22M to 35.2M as ART coverage climbed from 7% to 66%.

The chronic-condition signature. Effective treatment keeps people alive — locking in a permanent, growing care burden.
People living with HIV — rising burden
ART coverage vs. death rate (r ≈ −0.48)

Across 108 countries, higher ART coverage tracks lower death rates.

Treatment works. The negative correlation is the core evidence that programmes save lives.

Africa carries ~75% of the burden (26.3M), led by South Africa (7.8M).

Burden ≠ death rate. The Eastern Mediterranean (3.81%) and Western Pacific (2.71%) post the highest death rates.
Burden & death rate by WHO region
High-burden countries with ART ≤ 50%

Some high-burden countries still report sub-50% coverage.

Priority targets: Pakistan (16%), Sudan (20%), Madagascar (29%), Congo (34%), Indonesia (41%).
Project Brief

All 12 business questions, answered

1

How many new infections over time?

Fell 59% — 2.40M (2000) → 0.98M (2024); now plateauing.

2

Total HIV-related deaths globally?

~523K in 2024; down ~70% from the 2004 peak.

3

How many people living with HIV?

~35.2M in the latest year and rising.

4

Overall treatment coverage?

~66% average / ~79% PLHIV-weighted ART coverage (2024).

5

Mortality & survival rate?

Death rate ≈ 1.49%; survival rate ≈ 98.51%.

6

Highest deaths per 1,000 infections?

Lesotho, Ethiopia, Malawi, Zimbabwe, Thailand.

7

Infections vs deaths trend?

Both falling; deaths have fallen faster than infections.

8

Most affected regions?

Africa (~75% of burden); top death rates in E. Mediterranean & W. Pacific.

9

Best treatment access?

Rwanda, Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, Eswatini, Zambia (90%+).

10

Do high-infection countries have coverage?

Mostly yes (S. Africa, Nigeria 80%+) — but Indonesia 41%, Philippines 40% lag.

11

ART coverage vs mortality?

Negative correlation (r ≈ −0.48): more coverage, lower mortality.

12

Most at-risk countries?

Pakistan 16%, Sudan 20%, Madagascar 29%, Congo 34%, Indonesia 41%.

Dashboard Insights

Reading between the numbers

What the analysis tells us about burden, treatment and the systems that deliver care.

Dashboard Insight
▸

Across 2000–2024 the dashboard counts 690M+ people living with HIV. Rising prevalence isn’t worsening infections — better ART keeps patients alive, so HIV is increasingly a manageable chronic condition. Higher prevalence can reflect healthcare success.

▸

African countries carry the highest HIV burden — high infection rates, large numbers living with HIV and significant mortality — reflecting how healthcare inequality, poverty and limited treatment access affect outcomes across the region.

▸

In many countries death rates are falling faster than infection rates — evidence that healthcare systems and HIV interventions are improving survival through better access to medication and long-term care.

Dashboard Insight
▸

Countries with stronger ART coverage generally show lower mortality, better survival and improved long-term disease management. As treatment access improves, mortality tends to fall.

▸

Yet some countries record high “Deaths per 1,000 New Infections” even where coverage looks moderate or high — pointing to weak healthcare delivery, late diagnosis, poor follow-up, medication interruptions or data-reporting gaps.

▸

Treatment availability alone is not enough if implementation systems are weak — strong delivery, monitoring and reporting are as critical as access.

Recommendations

From insight to action

Expand access, prevention & early diagnosis

Reducing the global HIV burden requires early diagnosis, improved treatment access, stronger healthcare systems and effective prevention. Governments and healthcare organizations should expand community testing, awareness campaigns and mobile screening to encourage early detection and reduce transmission. Increasing access to affordable ART — particularly in high-burden regions such as Sub-Saharan Africa — is essential to lowering HIV-related deaths and improving quality of life.

Strengthen systems, monitoring & data

Healthcare systems must strengthen patient monitoring, treatment distribution and data-reporting to ensure consistent care and informed decisions. Preventive education, condom distribution and youth-awareness initiatives remain critical to reducing new infections. Tools such as Microsoft Power BI help monitor trends, identify high-risk populations and support evidence-based policymaking. Sustainable progress depends on integrating prevention, treatment, accurate reporting and data-driven strategies.

1
Close coverage gaps

Fund low-ART, high-burden countries first.

2
Re-energise prevention

PrEP, testing & education as infections plateau.

3
Fund the care pool

Sustainable, long-term ART financing.

4
Target retention hotspots

Diagnosis, adherence & viral suppression.

5
Modernise reporting

Annual data everywhere for faster action.

The Bottom Line

A public-health success in motion — with unfinished business

Infections down 59%, deaths down 70% from peak, treatment up ten-fold. Sustained, targeted, data-driven investment is what keeps the curve falling.
